title: 9 Best Herbal Teas For Premenstrual Syndrome
intro: Herbal teas for Premenstrual syndrome are a natural and holistic approach to alleviate the symptoms associated with this common women's health issue. Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) is a condition characterized by a range of physical and emotional symptoms that occur in the days leading up to menstruation, including bloating, mood swings, and breast tenderness. Herbal teas can effectively treat PMS due to their ability to regulate hormones, reduce inflammation, and promote relaxation, making them an attractive alternative to conventional medications. Examples of herbal teas that can help alleviate PMS symptoms include Peppermint tea, which eases digestive issues and reduces cramps; Ginger tea, which reduces inflammation and alleviates nausea; Chamomile tea, which promotes relaxation and calms anxiety; Raspberry leaf tea, which regulates hormones and reduces menstrual cramps; and Red clover tea, which eases hot flashes and breast tenderness.

preparations.herb_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas have been traditionally used to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), a common condition affecting many women. The herbal preparation's properties, including its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, help to reduce symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness. The bioactive constituents of Ginkgo biloba, including flavonoids, terpenoids, and bilobalide, have been shown to modulate neurotransmitters and hormones, which helps to regulate menstrual cycles and alleviate symptoms of PMS. The benefits of Ginkgo biloba teas in treating PMS include reduced mood swings, improved sleep quality, and a decrease in menstrual cramp severity, making it a popular natural remedy for women seeking alternative relief from PMS symptoms.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water, 1 teaspoon of dried Ginkgo biloba leaves, and a tea infuser or strainer.
Measure 1 teaspoon of dried Ginkgo biloba leaves and place them in the tea infuser or strainer.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the Ginkgo biloba leaves in the infuser or strainer.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, then remove the infuser or strainer.
Drink the tea and consider adding honey or lemon to taste, 2-3 times a day as needed for premenstrual syndrome relief.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Disease-a-month : DM", Ginkgo biloba teas may help improve premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Ginkgo biloba teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS), but possible side effects may include dizziness, nausea, and stomach upset due to its blood-thinning properties, as well as allergic reactions or interactions with certain medications. To minimize side effects, individuals should start with a low dose, monitor their body's response, and avoid consuming Ginkgo biloba teas with other medications, especially blood thinners, and during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
preparation_usage: Consult a doctor before using Ginkgo biloba tea for premenstrual syndrome.
Start with 1 cup of Ginkgo biloba tea, 2-3 times a day, for 7 days.
Monitor symptoms and adjust dosage to 1-2 cups, 2-3 times a day, if needed.
Continue using Ginkgo biloba tea throughout the menstrual cycle, adjusting dosage as needed.
Track symptoms and adjust usage after 3 months to optimize benefits.

preparations.herb_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas, made from the flowers of the lavender plant, have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their calming and relaxing properties. The bioactive constituents of Lavandula angustifolia, including linalool, linalyl acetate, and camphor, help to reduce anxiety, promote better sleep, and alleviate symptoms of PMS such as mood swings and breast tenderness. By consuming Lavandula angustifolia teas, women can experience a reduction in symptoms associated with PMS, including emotional instability and water retention, leading to an overall improvement in their quality of life. The benefits of using Lavandula angustifolia teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms, improved mood, and enhanced relaxation, making it a popular natural remedy for women experiencing this common condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers, 1 cup boiling water, and a strainer.
Measure 1 cup of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers into a heat-resistant cup.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the dried flowers. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into another cup using a strainer. Discard the flowers.
Drink the tea as needed, ideally 2-3 cups per day, to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Lavandula angustifolia teas can be used to treat the premenstrual syndrome ailment, but consuming it may cause side effects such as dizziness, headaches, and stomach upset due to its sedative and anti-inflammatory properties. Precautions should be taken when using this herbal preparation, including avoiding it during pregnancy and breastfeeding, and being cautious when driving or operating heavy machinery due to its potential to cause drowsiness.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Lavandula angustifolia tea 2-3 times a day.
Start 2-5 days before expected menstrual period for best results.
Use 1-2 teaspoons of dried flowers per cup of boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain and drink.
Consult doctor before using tea if you have severe PMS symptoms.

preparations.herb_desc: Paeonia lactiflora teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their soothing and calming properties. The flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins present in this herbal preparation help to alleviate PMS symptoms by reducing inflammation, modulating hormonal balance, and promoting relaxation. The bioactive constituents, including paeonol, paeoniflorin, and benzoyl paeoniflorin, exert their effects by inhibiting the production of prostaglandins, which are known to contribute to PMS symptoms such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings. By using Paeonia lactiflora teas, individuals can experience relief from PMS symptoms, improved mood, and reduced stress levels, making it a beneficial herbal remedy for managing this common gynecological condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 cups of fresh Paeonia lactiflora flowers and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the Paeonia lactiflora flowers in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large cup.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to sweeten the tea, if desired.
Drink 1 cup of the tea 2-3 times a day to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Journal of midwifery & women's health", Paeonia lactiflora teas, along with calcium, vitamin B6, and chasteberry, have some level of support for the treatment of premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Paeonia lactiflora teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms, but possible side effects may include drowsiness, dizziness, and stomach upset due to its sedative and antispasmodic properties. To use Paeonia lactiflora teas safely, it is recommended to start with small doses and gradually increase as needed, avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while consuming it, and be cautious when combining it with other medications, especially sedatives or antidepressants.
preparation_usage: Take 1-2 teaspoons of Paeonia lactiflora tea powder 3 times a day.
Drink 1 cup of the tea 1-2 hours before meals.
Start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed.
Continue using the tea for 3-5 days before menstruation starts.
Monitor symptoms and adjust the dosage as needed.

preparations.herb_desc: Valeriana officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat the premenstrual syndrome (PMS) ailment due to its calming and sedative properties. The herbal preparation contains bioactive constituents such as valerenic acid and valepotriates, which help to reduce anxiety and alleviate symptoms associated with PMS, such as irritability and mood swings. By promoting relaxation and reducing stress, Valeriana officinalis teas can help to regulate menstrual cycles and alleviate symptoms of PMS, providing relief to women experiencing this condition. The benefits of using Valeriana officinalis teas to treat PMS include reduced anxiety and stress levels, improved sleep quality, and a decrease in symptoms associated with PMS, making it a natural and effective remedy for this common women's health issue.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water and 1 tablespoon of dried Valeriana officinalis root.
Steep the root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then strain it.
In a separate cup, combine 1 cup of boiling water and 1 teaspoon of dried Valeriana officinalis root.
Steep the root for 5-7 minutes, then strain it. Combine the two tea mixtures.
Drink the tea mixture 2-3 times a day, 1-2 days before your period starts, to help ease premenstrual symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Disease-a-month : DM", Valeriana officinalis teas may improve premenstrual syndrome symptoms, with more study needed on its long-term safety and efficacy.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Valeriana officinalis teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome, but it may cause drowsiness, stomach upset, and interactions with certain medications due to its sedative properties. To minimize potential side effects, it's recommended to start with small doses and gradually increase as needed, avoid consuming the tea before driving or operating heavy machinery, and monitor your body's response to the herbal preparation.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Valeriana officinalis tea 2-3 times a day.
Start with 1 teaspoon of dried root per cup of boiling water.
Gradually increase to 2 teaspoons if needed, after 2-3 days.
Use Valeriana officinalis tea for 5-7 days before and during menstrual cycle.
Consult healthcare provider for personalized dosage and frequency.

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, also known as ginger tea, have been traditionally used to alleviate the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties of this herbal preparation help to treat PMS by reducing cramps, bloating, and mood swings. The bioactive constituents, such as gingerols and shogaols, present in Zingiber officinale teas, have been shown to inhibit the production of prostaglandins, which are hormone-like substances responsible for menstrual cramps. The benefits of using Zingiber officinale teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and fatigue, as well as improved overall well-being and quality of life.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Zingiber officinale (Ginger root) and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the ginger root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Strain the mixture.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the ginger tea, if desired for taste.
Drink 1 cup of the ginger tea 2-3 times a day when experiencing premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
Consult a healthcare professional before using ginger tea as a treatment for premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Anaesthesia", Zingiber officinale teas for premenstrual syndrome may be effective in reducing nausea, as a study found that ginger root significantly reduced the number of incidences of nausea compared to a placebo in women who had major gynaecological surgery.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be beneficial in alleviating symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but some individuals may experience side effects such as nausea, dizziness, and stomach upset due to its ginger content. To minimize potential side effects, it's recommended to start with a small dosage and gradually increase as needed, and also to avoid consuming Zingiber officinale teas on an empty stomach to reduce the risk of stomach upset.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Zingiber officinale tea 2-3 times daily as needed.
Start with 1 teaspoon of dried ginger per cup of boiling water.
Gradually increase to 2 teaspoons per cup for stronger relief.
Continue usage for 7-10 days before and during menstrual cycle.
Consult a healthcare professional for personalized dosage and guidance.

preparations.herb_desc: Passiflora incarnata teas, also known as passionflower teas, have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their sedative and anti-anxiety properties. The bioactive constituents of passionflower, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, help to regulate mood, reduce stress, and alleviate symptoms associated with PMS, such as irritability and anxiety. The sedative properties of passionflower tea help to calm the nervous system, promoting relaxation and reducing symptoms of PMS. Regular consumption of passionflower tea may also help to regulate hormonal imbalances and improve overall well-being, making it a beneficial herbal preparation for women experiencing PMS.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Passiflora incarnata leaves and flowers.
Combine the dried herbs with 1 cup of boiling water in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the herbs to release their properties.
Strain the tea into another cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth.
Drink 1/2 to 1 cup of the tea, 2-3 times a day, as needed to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Disease-a-month : DM", Passiflora incarnata teas may improve premenstrual syndrome, however, more study is needed on this herb.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Passiflora incarnata teas can be used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), but possible side effects may include drowsiness, dizziness, and nausea due to its sedative properties. To minimize potential side effects, it is essential to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, and also to avoid consuming Passiflora incarnata teas with other sedatives or substances that can exacerbate drowsiness.
preparation_usage: Consult a healthcare professional before using Passiflora incarnata tea
Start with 1/2 cup of tea 2-3 times a week, observe effects and adjust
Gradually increase to 1 cup of tea 2-3 times a week as needed
Take tea 1-2 hours before expected PMS symptoms, or as directed by doctor
Monitor symptoms and adjust dosage or frequency with healthcare professional guidance

preparations.herb_desc: Vitex agnus-castus teas have been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), a common condition affecting women of reproductive age. The herbal preparation's properties, including its adaptogenic and anti-inflammatory effects, help to regulate hormonal imbalances and reduce symptoms such as mood swings, breast tenderness, and bloating. The bioactive constituents present in Vitex agnus-castus teas, including iridoids like agnuside and aucubin, act as estrogen modulators and have been shown to help balance the body's estrogen levels, thereby alleviating PMS symptoms. The benefits of using Vitex agnus-castus teas for PMS include reduced severity and frequency of symptoms, improved mood and overall well-being, and a natural, non-pharmacological approach to managing PMS.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Vitex agnus-castus leaves and flowers.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Remove the pot from heat and add the dried Vitex agnus-castus.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the mixture.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, 1-2 weeks before your period starts.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Archives of Gynecology and Obstetrics", Vitex agnus-castus teas for premenstrual syndrome were found to be effective, with patients showing a significant reduction in PMS-related symptoms, as measured by a 42.5% decrease in the Moos' menstrual distress questionnaire (MMDQ) score.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Vitex agnus-castus teas can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but some users may experience side effects such as dizziness, headaches, and nausea due to the herbal preparation's potential interaction with certain medications or exacerbation of underlying health conditions. When using Vitex agnus-castus teas to treat premenstrual syndrome, it is essential to note that they may cause allergic reactions, particularly in individuals with allergies to plants in the Vitex family, and to avoid consuming them during pregnancy or while breastfeeding due to the potential risks associated with hormonal fluctuations.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Vitex agnus-castus tea 2-3 times a day.
Start with 1 teaspoon of dried herb per cup of boiling water.
Gradually increase to 2 teaspoons per cup if symptoms persist.
Use tea for 7-10 days before menstruation to alleviate symptoms.
Consult a healthcare professional for personalized dosage and advice.

preparations.herb_desc: Angelica sinensis teas have been traditionally used to treat the premenstrual syndrome (PMS) ailment, leveraging its properties as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant agent. The herbal preparation helps to alleviate symptoms of PMS by regulating hormonal balance, reducing anxiety and stress, and calming menstrual cramps. Bioactive constituents such as ferulic acid, sinensiside, and ligustilide in Angelica sinensis teas are responsible for its therapeutic effects, including anti-inflammatory and estrogen-mimicking properties. The benefits of using Angelica sinensis teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms of bloating, mood swings, and fatigue, as well as improved overall well-being and quality of life.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 teaspoons of dried Angelica sinensis root and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the Angelica sinensis root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid into a cup and discard the root.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired. The recommended dose is 1 cup, 2-3 times a day.
Drink the tea 2-3 days before your period to alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "The journal of sexual medicine", Angelica sinensis teas may be suggested for arousal disorder studies, which could potentially help alleviate symptoms associated with premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Angelica sinensis teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome, but may cause side effects such as dizziness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions in some individuals. To safely use this herbal preparation, it is recommended to start with small amounts and monitor body reactions, and to avoid using it if you have any underlying health conditions or are pregnant.
preparation_usage: Consult a healthcare professional for guidance on using Angelica sinensis tea.
Start with 1 teaspoon of dried Angelica sinensis root per cup of boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea before consumption.
Drink 1-2 cups of Angelica sinensis tea 2-3 times a day, 7-10 days before menstruation.
Monitor your symptoms and adjust the dosage or frequency as advised by your healthcare professional.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas, rich in anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, have been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The bioactive constituents, including glycyrrhizin, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, help to reduce inflammation, modulate hormone levels, and regulate mood, thereby alleviating symptoms of PMS. The antispasmodic and antacids properties of these teas also help to soothe cramps and digestive issues associated with PMS. The benefits of using Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to treat PMS include reduced anxiety, improved mood, and a decrease in menstrual cramp intensity, making it a valuable herbal remedy for women experiencing PMS.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 teaspoons of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root and a tea infuser.
Boil 1 cup of water in a kettle.
Pour the boiling water over the dried root in the tea infuser.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then remove the infuser.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help with premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Disease-a-month : DM", Glycyrrhiza glabra teas may help improve premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can be used to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as bloating and mood swings, but may cause side effects like increased blood pressure, fluid retention, and potassium deficiency due to its licorice root content. Precautions should be taken when using these teas, including limiting consumption to small amounts, avoiding use for extended periods, and being mindful of interactions with other medications that may also affect blood pressure or electrolyte balance.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Glycyrrhiza glabra tea 2-3 times a day as needed
Start with 1 tablespoon of dried root per cup of boiling water
Gradually increase dosage to 2 tablespoons per cup if needed
Use tea 3-5 days before onset of menstruation for best results
Consult a healthcare professional if symptoms persist or worsen

preparations.herb_desc: Avena sativa teas have been traditionally used to treat the premenstrual syndrome (PMS) ailment, offering relief from symptoms such as anxiety, irritability, and mood swings. The sedative and anxiolytic properties of Avena sativa teas help to calm the nervous system, promoting relaxation and reducing stress levels. The bioactive constituents, including avenacosides, avenanthramides, and ferulic acid, contribute to the tea's therapeutic effects by acting as antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents. By promoting relaxation, reducing anxiety, and alleviating symptoms, Avena sativa teas offer a natural and beneficial approach to treating PMS, providing relief and improving overall well-being.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Avena sativa (oat straw) and a tea infuser.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot or kettle.
Add 2-3 teaspoons of dried Avena sativa to the tea infuser.
Steep the Avena sativa in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then remove the infuser.
Strain and drink the tea 2-3 times a day for relief from premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Disease-a-month : DM, Avena sativa teas may help improve the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Avena sativa teas can be an effective herbal remedy for alleviating premenstrual syndrome symptoms, but potential side effects include drowsiness, stomach upset, and increased heart rate. To minimize risks, users should start with low doses, avoid consuming it before bedtime, and be cautious when combining it with other stimulants or medications, as it may interact with them and exacerbate symptoms.

preparations.herb_desc: Angelica archangelica teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and estrogen-regulating properties. The bioactive constituents of Angelica archangelica, including ferulic acid, apiol, and ligustilide, help to alleviate PMS symptoms by reducing inflammation, relaxing uterine spasms, and regulating hormonal imbalances. Regular consumption of Angelica archangelica teas may help to alleviate symptoms associated with PMS, including bloating, breast tenderness, and mood swings, thereby improving overall quality of life. The benefits of using Angelica archangelica teas to treat PMS include reduced symptom severity, improved hormonal balance, and a natural, non-pharmacological approach to managing menstrual health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Angelica archangelica roots and 2 cups of water.
Rinse the Angelica archangelica roots with cold water and chop them into small pieces.
Combine the chopped roots and water in a saucepan and bring to a boil over high heat.
Reduce heat to low and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes, then strain the mixture.
Drink 1/2 cup of the tea, 2-3 times a day, as needed to relieve premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of Midwifery & Women's Health, Angelica archangelica teas, along with calcium, vitamin B6, and chasteberry, have a higher level of support from randomized controlled trial evidence for the treatment of premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Angelica archangelica teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome, but it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, dizziness, and stomach upset in some individuals. To safely use this herbal preparation, one should be cautious of interactions with other medications and consider potential interactions with other herbal supplements, and also be aware of the possible risk of exacerbating certain underlying medical conditions.

preparations.herb_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to its analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties. The bioactive constituents, including ferulic acid, sesquiterpenes, and lignans, help to alleviate symptoms such as bloating, mood swings, and cramps associated with PMS. These bioactive constituents interact with the body's neurotransmitters, particularly serotonin, to reduce anxiety and alleviate symptoms of PMS. By consuming Cimicifuga racemosa teas, women can experience relief from PMS symptoms, improved mood, and reduced anxiety, making it a beneficial herbal remedy for this common health issue.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Cimicifuga racemosa root and 2 cups of water in a pot.
Bring the water to a boil, then reduce heat to low and add the dried root.
Let it simmer for 5-7 minutes, then turn off the heat and let it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve, discarding the root.
Add honey to taste, if desired, and drink 1/2 to 1 cup of the tea 2-3 times a day when symptoms occur.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Molecules (Basel, Switzerland), Cimicifuga racemosa teas for premenstrual syndrome are not specifically mentioned in the study as a treatment option, but black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa) was shown to help reduce menopausal symptoms, which may include symptoms associated with premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cimicifuga racemosa teas can cause dizziness and nausea in some individuals when used to treat premenstrual syndrome, especially if consumed in large quantities or combined with other herbal remedies. It is recommended to start with a small dose and gradually increase it to minimize the risk of side effects such as headaches, stomach upset, and changes in menstrual flow, and to avoid using it during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

preparations.herb_desc: Melissa officinalis teas, also known as lemon balm tea, have been traditionally used to alleviate the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The anti-anxiety and relaxing properties of Melissa officinalis teas help to calm the nervous system and reduce stress, which is often a contributing factor to PMS. The bioactive constituents, including rosmarinic acid, flavonoids, and terpenes, have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help to reduce symptoms such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings associated with PMS. By consuming Melissa officinalis teas, women can experience a range of benefits, including improved mood, reduced anxiety, and enhanced sleep quality, making it a popular natural remedy for PMS relief.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Melissa officinalis leaves or 1/2 cup of dried leaves.
Heat 1 cup of water in a pot until it boils.
Add the Melissa officinalis leaves to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the leaves. Add honey or sugar to taste.
Drink 1-2 cups of the tea per day, 2-3 days before your period is expected to start, and continue until symptoms subside.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of midwifery & women's health, Melissa officinalis teas for premenstrual syndrome is not listed among the therapies with higher levels of support from randomized controlled trial evidence, but rather, calcium, vitamin B(6), and chasteberry are recommended.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melissa officinalis teas can be used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but possible side effects may include drowsiness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions in some individuals. To minimize risks, it is essential to consume the tea in moderation, avoid combining it with other sedatives or medications, and monitor personal reactions to the tea's active compounds.

preparations.herb_desc: Cinnamomum verum teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antispasmodic properties. These properties help to alleviate symptoms of PMS such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation. The bioactive constituents of Cinnamomum verum teas, including cinnamaldehyde and eugenol, are responsible for their therapeutic effects, which help to regulate menstrual cycles and reduce symptoms of PMS. The benefits of using Cinnamomum verum teas to treat PMS include reduced severity of symptoms, improved mood, and enhanced overall well-being.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Cinnamomum verum (Ceylon cinnamon) bark, 1 cup of boiling water, and a tea infuser.
Steep the Ceylon cinnamon in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid using a tea infuser or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey (optional) to the tea and stir well.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, or as needed, to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Daru : journal of Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Cinnamomum verum teas may have beneficial effects on improving menstrual and ovulatory dysfunctions, a symptom associated with premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cinnamomum verum teas can be used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms, but potential side effects may include stomach upset, nausea, and allergic reactions such as skin rashes or itching in some individuals. Precautions should be taken to avoid excessive consumption, as it may cause liver damage or interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, and to monitor blood sugar levels in individuals with diabetes.

preparations.herb_desc: Scutellaria baicalensis teas, also known as Baikal skullcap, have been traditionally used to treat the premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their unique properties that help alleviate symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with PMS. The bioactive constituents of Scutellaria baicalensis, including baicalin, wogonin, and wogonoside, play a crucial role in treating PMS by modulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Regular consumption of Scutellaria baicalensis teas has been shown to provide numerous benefits in treating PMS, including improved mood, reduced symptoms of anxiety and depression, and enhanced overall quality of life.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water and 1 tablespoon of dried Scutellaria baicalensis root.
Measure 2 tablespoons of dried Scutellaria baicalensis root for a stronger tea, if needed.
Steep the dried root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the root.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a week, 1-2 cups per serving, to help with premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of midwifery & women's health, Scutellaria baicalensis teas are not specifically mentioned as a treatment for premenstrual syndrome, but chasteberry is listed as a therapy with higher support from randomized controlled trial evidence for this condition.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Scutellaria baicalensis teas can be used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but it may cause side effects such as stomach upset, dizziness, and allergic reactions in some individuals, particularly when consumed in large quantities or combined with other herbal preparations. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to brew the tea with cool water, drink it in moderation, and be aware of any sensitivity or intolerance to the plant's active compounds.

preparations.herb_desc: Schisandra chinensis teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their adaptogenic and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Schisandra chinensis, including schisandrins, lignans, and polysaccharides, help to regulate hormonal imbalances and reduce symptoms of PMS such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness. By modulating the body's stress response and improving sleep quality, Schisandra chinensis teas help to alleviate symptoms of PMS and promote overall well-being. The benefits of using Schisandra chinensis teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms, improved mood, and a decrease in stress levels, making it a natural and effective herbal remedy for women experiencing PMS.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water and 1 tablespoon of dried Schisandra chinensis.
Add the dried Schisandra chinensis to a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup.
Pour the boiling water over the Schisandra chinensis and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into another cup using the tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a week to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Disease-a-month : DM, Schisandra chinensis teas may help improve premenstrual syndrome symptoms, but more study is needed.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Schisandra chinensis teas can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but it may also cause side effects such as dizziness, headaches, and stomach upset in some individuals, particularly if taken in large quantities or for extended periods. To minimize potential risks, it is recommended to consume Schisandra chinensis teas in moderation and avoid combining them with other medications or substances that may interact with their active compounds.

preparations.herb_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat the premenstrual syndrome (PMS) ailment, leveraging the herb's anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and adaptogenic properties to alleviate symptoms. The bioactive constituents of Rosmarinus officinalis, including camphor, borneol, and bornyl acetate, help to regulate hormonal imbalances and reduce stress, thereby mitigating PMS symptoms such as anxiety, mood swings, and breast tenderness. By promoting relaxation and reducing inflammation, Rosmarinus officinalis teas can help to alleviate PMS symptoms and improve overall well-being. The benefits of using Rosmarinus officinalis teas for PMS include reduced anxiety and stress, improved mood, and enhanced overall quality of life.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Rosmarinus officinalis leaves or 1 teaspoon of dried leaves.
Boil 1 cup of water in a kettle or on the stove.
Add the Rosmarinus officinalis leaves to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, or as needed, to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Disease-a-month : DM, Rosmarinus officinalis teas may help improve premenstrual syndrome, but more study is needed on this herb.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Rosmarinus officinalis teas can be beneficial in treating premenstrual syndrome, but some possible side effects include dizziness, nausea, and stomach upset, especially when consumed in high doses or with certain medications. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, and also to avoid consuming Rosmarinus officinalis teas with antacids, blood thinners, or other medications that may interact with its active compounds.

preparations.herb_desc: Ligusticum chuanxiong teas have been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their ability to regulate menstrual cycles and alleviate symptoms such as bloating, mood swings, and breast tenderness. The properties of Ligusticum chuanxiong teas that contribute to this effect include their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress associated with PMS. The bioactive constituents of Ligusticum chuanxiong teas, including ligustilide and senkyunolide, are responsible for their therapeutic effects, which include pain relief, anti-anxiety, and anti-inflammatory activities. The benefits of using Ligusticum chuanxiong teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms, improved mood, and enhanced overall well-being, making it a valuable herbal remedy for women experiencing this common gynecological condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Ligusticum chuanxiong root. You can buy it from a health store or online.
Add 1 cup of boiling water to a teapot. Pour in the dried Ligusticum chuanxiong root.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes. Let it cool down for a few minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer. Discard the solids.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, 1-2 weeks before your period. You can also add honey to taste.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of midwifery & women's health, Ligusticum chuanxiong teas are not mentioned as a treatment option for premenstrual syndrome in the given study, however, chasteberry, calcium, vitamin B(6) are mentioned as therapies with a higher level of support for premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Ligusticum chuanxiong teas can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, including bloating and mood swings, but may also cause side effects such as dizziness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions in some individuals. To use Ligusticum chuanxiong teas safely, individuals should start with a low dose, gradually increase as needed, and monitor their body's response to the herbal preparation, as well as avoid consuming it if they have a history of bleeding disorders or are taking anticoagulant medications.

preparations.herb_desc: Matricaria chamomilla teas have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to their anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and sedative properties. The bioactive constituents of Matricaria chamomilla, including apigenin, luteolin, and chamazulene, help to alleviate symptoms of PMS by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation. By reducing anxiety and promoting a good night's sleep, Matricaria chamomilla teas can help to alleviate symptoms of PMS, including mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness. The benefits of using Matricaria chamomilla teas to treat PMS include reduced symptoms, improved mood, and overall well-being, making it a popular natural remedy for women experiencing PMS symptoms.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried Matricaria chamomilla flowers.
Heat 1 cup of water in a pot.
Pour the heated water over the dried flowers in a cup.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid.
Drink 1/2 cup of the tea 2-3 times a day when experiencing premenstrual symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of midwifery & women's health, Matricaria chamomilla teas for premenstrual syndrome is not specifically mentioned in the study as a treatment option, however, the study suggests that calcium, vitamin B(6), and chasteberry have some level of support for premenstrual syndrome treatment.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Matricaria chamomilla teas can be used as a natural remedy to alleviate the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), but possible side effects may include allergic reactions, drowsiness, and interactions with certain medications, particularly blood thinners and sedatives. When using Matricaria chamomilla teas to treat PMS, it is essential to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, and to avoid consuming it in large quantities, especially if you are pregnant or breastfeeding.

preparations.herb_desc: Mentha x piperita teas, derived from the peppermint plant, have been traditionally used to treat premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms, including bloating, anxiety, and mood swings. The bioactive constituents, including menthone, menthol, and limonene, possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help alleviate PMS symptoms by reducing prostaglandins and other inflammatory mediators. By promoting relaxation, reducing stress, and alleviating digestive discomfort, Mentha x piperita teas can provide relief from PMS symptoms, allowing women to maintain their daily activities and overall well-being. The benefits of using Mentha x piperita teas to treat PMS include reduced severity of symptoms, improved mood, and enhanced overall quality of life.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh peppermint leaves (Mentha x piperita) and 1 cup of boiling water.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to a cup for sweetness (optional).
Pour the boiling water over the peppermint leaves in a heat-resistant cup.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the leaves before drinking.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day for relief from premenstrual syndrome symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Disease-a-month : DM, Mentha x piperita teas, specifically peppermint tea, may be beneficial in alleviating the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Mentha x piperita teas can be used to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, but its consumption may cause side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and headaches in some individuals due to its high menthol content. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to start with small doses and gradually increase the amount, as well as to avoid consuming the tea on an empty stomach and with excessive amounts of sugar or other stimulants.

preparation_study: According to Journal of midwifery & women's health, Ginkgo biloba teas are not specifically recommended for premenstrual syndrome based on the study, as the therapies with a higher level of support from randomized controlled trial evidence for premenstrual syndrome include calcium, vitamin B(6), and chasteberry.
intro_study: According to "BMJ (Clinical research ed.)", teas for premenstrual syndrome may be beneficial as the study suggests that Agnus castus fruit extract is an effective treatment for relieving symptoms of premenstrual syndrome.
